content.problemStatement:
Chef has a binary string SSS. He can modify it by choosing any subsequence of length 333 from it and deleting the **first** and **last** character of the subsequence.

For example, if S=110101S = \textcolor{red}{11}01\textcolor{red}{0}1S=110101, Chef can choose the subsequence marked in red and delete its first and last characters, obtaining the string S=1011S = 1011S=1011.

Chef wonders what is the lexicographically largest string he can obtain by modifying the original string using a finite number of operations. Please help Chef in this task.

**Note:** A binary string AAA is said to be lexicographically larger than another binary string BBB if:


BBB is a proper prefix of AAA (for example, 101101101 is lexicographically larger than 101010); or
There exists an index iii such that A1=B1,A2=B2,…,Ai−1=Bi−1A_1 = B_1, A_2 = B_2, \ldots, A_{i-1} = B_{i-1}A1​=B1​,A2​=B2​,…,Ai−1​=Bi−1​ and Ai>BiA_i \gt B_iAi​>Bi​.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single line of input containing the original binary string SSS.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the lexicographically largest string Chef can obtain.
constraints: SSS is a binary string, i.e, only contains the characters 000 and 111 in it
The sum of ∣S∣|S|∣S∣ over all test cases won't exceed 3⋅1053\cdot 10^53⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: It is optimal to not perform any moves.
Test case 222: In one move, by choosing the subsequence 10101\textcolor{red}{010}1010, the string can be made into 111111, which is the lexicographically largest possible.
Test case 333: It is optimal to not perform any move.
